data.title: Why Dry January is a silly social campaign and ‘no-lo’ drinks are pointless
data.description:
Dry January is upon us again, a time of year when people who feel the need to join a hashtag movement promise to nobody in particular that they will abstain from alcohol for an entire month.
In other words, they’ll be engaging in performative brags on social media and being a total sanctimonious jerk to everybody else just to make us feel a bit more inferior.
That’s right. There is absolutely no reason for continuing this silly social campaign. Alcohol consumption is at an all-time low in most Western countries. The wine market is down internationally. Pubs are closing and more university-age youngsters are self-defining as non-drinkers.
We need Dry January like we need a new campaign discouraging doctors from using bloodsucking leeches as a first-choice treatment method. Honestly, as a social cause, Dry January is so 2019.
As evidence that boozing has fallen so precipitously from our vice radar, even Carlsberg has decided to jump on what is being termed the “no-lo” bandwagon, or no and low alcohol beverages.
The brewery’s Hong Kong office sent out a press release this week to say it is celebrating Dry January by launching a whole range of alcohol-free beverages for this month and beyond.
Among the products are Italian beer Poretti Zero, German beer Erdinger Alkoholfrei, Hong Kong pale ale Young Master Zero, and Carlsberg’s 0.0 beer. Beyond beer, there are mixers, tonics and a Hong Kong-inspired salted lime soda.

Carlsberg is clearly presenting itself as diversifying but, to me, it sure seems like they are turning their backs on their core business.
Worse, it feels like the same sort of short-sighted plan as when fast food chains tried to present themselves as health-conscious by offering meat-free burgers and plant-based nugget alternatives. How well are those options doing these days?
Real beer and wine connoisseurs will always want exemplary brews and fine vintages from their favourite producers. Folks who don’t drink will just order tea, coffee or maybe a Coke. If feeling trendy, they might splurge on a mocktail or a bottle of sparkling tea. But I highly doubt they will ask for no-lo beer or a zero-alcohol wine.
More likely, real beer lovers will be insulted by the alcohol-free beer options. The point is to encourage people to cut down, not replace their ale completely.
Mindful drinking is not abstinence; neither is it switching over to unappetising, watery versions of their pub draft. The fact is, most of us are not teetotallers. But we also don’t get smash-faced every weekend. It is not a binary choice to only be sober or only be drunk.
Anyway, the idea of avoiding booze for only a month is useless as a long-term resolve for changing and improving health, especially if it builds withdrawal symptoms so severe that people go on a bender on February 1.
I tend to think the campaign is as futile as a guy telling his girlfriend, “Honey, I’m not going to cheat on you this whole month. It’s going to do so much good for our relationship.”
Then again, this is mostly moot since drinking is as estranged to Gen Z as partying, dating, full-time work and talking to people face to face. To them, Dry January is just… January.

data.title: Trump sets 180-day deadline to counter China’s control of critical minerals
data.description:
US President Donald Trump has put allies and trade partners on notice, invoking national security powers to ease China’s chokehold on critical minerals, warning global suppliers to negotiate agreements to secure reliable, diversified supplies for the US or face new trade barriers, including tariffs.
In a proclamation signed on Wednesday, the “America first” president declared that the US’ reliance on foreign-processed critical minerals poses a national security threat.
He directed US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick to negotiate new or expanded agreements to “adjust imports” of processed critical minerals and their derivative products.


Negotiators have exactly 180 days, until July 13, 2026, to secure binding or enforceable agreements.
While the proclamation does not explicitly require countries to prove their minerals are mined and processed outside adversarial nations to avoid tariffs, it emphasises commitments that diversify supply chains away from dominant and potentially coercive sources.
Measures include boosting allied processing capacity, securing offtake agreements for US access, investing in non-Chinese facilities and using trade-stabilising tools such as price floors to counter price swings and volatility.
If no deal is reached within the deadline, the president is authorised to bypass further review and impose “remedial” measures, including high tariffs, quotas or mandatory “minimum import prices”.
China controls about 60 per cent of global rare earth mining and 90 per cent of processing, giving it significant leverage over materials such as rare earths, gallium and graphite.
“Mining a mineral domestically does not safeguard the national security of the United States if the United States remains dependent on a foreign country for the processing of that mineral,” the proclamation said.
Greer, in a statement issued shortly after the order, said: “It’s no secret we need more resilient supply chains for critical minerals.”
“By negotiating with interested parties to create an economically viable market for critical minerals, we can promote demand for and boost the supply of critical minerals at home and with partner countries,” he added.

Although allies, including Australia and Canada, mine many of these minerals, most are still shipped to China for refining.
Countries such as Malaysia and Indonesia are emerging as alternative processing hubs. Malaysia hosts the world’s largest non-Chinese rare earth processing plant, operated by Australia’s Lynas Rare Earths.
Australia and Japan signed a critical minerals deal with the US in October last year. While Malaysia and Thailand also have similar cooperation agreements.
Vietnam and Indonesia are expanding facilities for nickel, cobalt and other minerals, often with Western or allied partners to reduce Chinese influence, although some projects still involve Chinese investment.
By targeting “derivative products”, including rare earth magnets used in electric vehicle motors and processed lithium used in batteries, the order forces allies to choose between the Chinese processing industry and access to the US market.
The 180-day deadline puts immediate pressure on the European Union and India, both of which have been hesitant to fully commit to a “price floor” system that could raise costs for their manufacturers.
India is already facing hefty US tariffs of 75 per cent – 25 per cent reciprocal, 25 per cent on Russian crude, and 25 per cent on trade with Iran. No trade deal has been signed between the two sides yet.
The action follows a nine-month Section 232 investigation by the Commerce Department that found the US is dangerously dependent on foreign sources, particularly for the processing and refining of materials vital to defence systems, energy infrastructure and hi-tech manufacturing.
According to the report submitted to Trump in October, the United States was 100 per cent net-import reliant for 12 critical minerals in 2024 and at least 50 per cent reliant for another 29.
Even where the US has domestic mining capacity, including for rare earth elements, cobalt and nickel, it lacks sufficient processing infrastructure, forcing materials to be exported for refining before being reimported.
The Commerce Department concluded this dependence leaves US defence, aerospace, telecommunications and transport sectors vulnerable to supply disruptions, price volatility and foreign coercion.
Earlier this week, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent convened a high-level finance ministerial in Washington to address these vulnerabilities, with a focus on securing and diversifying critical mineral supply chains, particularly for rare earth elements.
The meeting included G7 finance ministers from the US, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan and Britain, as well as representatives from the European Union, Australia, India, Mexico and South Korea, expanding the group to key mining and manufacturing partners.

data.title: Japan’s Takaichi faces uphill task in lower house election after ally’s exit
data.description:
Komeito is emerging as a potential political kingmaker in Japan ahead of a widely expected snap lower house election, three months after the party affiliated with a religious movement announced it was ending an alliance with the ruling Liberal Democratic Party over differences with Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi.
On January 23, the opening day of the Diet, Takaichi is expected to announce the dissolution of the House of Representatives, with polling likely to take place on February 8 or February 15, according to media reports.
Takaichi has told the LDP of her plan to dissolve parliament for an election soon, and would reveal more details on such a move on Monday, according to Hirofumi Yoshimura, co-leader of the Japan Innovation Party, which is part of the ruling party’s coalition.
While the prime minister is riding high in opinion polls and has largely controlled the national narrative since taking office, some analysts warn that pressing ahead without Komeito’s backing could prove costly.
“I believe that calling the election now would be idiotic because it could cost Takaichi her premiership,” said Michael Cucek, a professor of Asian Studies at the Tokyo campus of Temple University.
Under the former alliance, Komeito voters were instructed to vote tactically in single-seat districts, mainly by backing LDP candidates and hence delivering an estimated additional 6 million votes to the ruling party nationwide at the last lower house election in 2024, Cucek said.
With Komeito’s departure, those votes would no longer flow to the LDP, he added, and the impact could be compounded by Komeito’s decision to cooperate with the centrist Constitutional Democratic Party of Japan (CDP).

Komeito leader Tetsuo Saito met with CDP chief Yoshihiko Noda on Monday, with the two men later confirming that they would cooperate “on a higher level” in the run-up to the next election for the 465-seat Lower House.
Noda, who served as prime minister in the Democratic Party of Japan-led government for 14 months until December 2012, was bullish about the CDP’s chances in the coming vote, telling reporters on Sunday: “We will devote our efforts to changing the government.”
While Noda admitted that securing a simple majority would be “difficult”, when there were so many political parties competing for votes, he said the CDP-Komeito alliance was set to become the single largest force in the chamber.
“It is the reformist, centrist political forces that will replace the LDP,” he said.
Still, Takaichi has done better than expected since she took office in October last year, presiding over a party that was trounced in the upper house election in July and a minority government, according to analysts.
“She has maintained a pretty high public support rate since she became prime minister, and that has not changed even after she came under pressure from China regarding her comments in November regarding Taiwan,” said Tadashi Anno, a professor of politics at Tokyo’s Sophia University.

Takaichi angered mainland China when she told the Diet in November that a “contingency” around Taiwan could pose a threat to Japan and require a military response.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
A poll released by national broadcaster NHK on Monday showed Takaichi’s cabinet had a support rate of 62 per cent. Of those, 33 per cent said the cabinet was making progress on policies, 25 per cent said it was better than the alternative parties, and 21 per cent said they had high hopes for Japan’s future.
Opposition parties had not been able to attack Takaichi on her Taiwan stance out of concern they would be accused of being “unpatriotic or an agent of China,” Anno said.
Similarly, plans by Takaichi’s administration to curb soaring price rises and large-scale immigration would appeal to the electorate and win back many voters who were frustrated at what they perceived as the LDP’s failures on these issues and went over to Sanseito in last July’s election, he said, referring to the ultra-right-wing party.
“With these policies, she has arguably stolen the show,” Anno added.
In the October 2024 lower house election, the LDP lost 68 seats while Sanseito won three seats. Sanseito did even better in the July 2025 upper house election, winning 14 seats, up from just one previously.


But Anno cautioned that the LDP could not just rely on winning over those who voted for Sanseito previously.
“Takaichi is doing well in the polls, but the LDP as a party does not have the same support rate,” he pointed out. “It is hard to predict what will happen, but I do sense that many Japanese are worried about the economic pressure that China is applying, which might make some voters resist Takaichi’s hardline stance and turn to parties that are more conciliatory towards Beijing.”
Cucek was pessimistic about Takaichi’s electoral outlook.
“The LDP has lost the 6 million votes from Komeito that it relied on in the last election, so how can they replace that number?” he said.
While the LDP claims that it intends to appeal to younger voters, who turned out in greater numbers in recent elections and many of whom have expressed support for Takaichi’s policies, it would not be able to “energise” 6 million young Japanese, according to Cucek.
A potential strategy by Takaichi was to win back disaffected LDP voters who backed Sanseito last time, but the ruling party only received 1.8 million ballots from these voters in the 2025 election, leaving it far short of the 6 million it needed to maintain its strength in the chamber, Cucek said.
“The LDP’s position is that the polls show the public is firmly behind her and the party is connecting with people who voted for other parties – both far-right and centrist – in the last election because Takaichi is popular, but I’m not sure that conversion will happen.”
Additional reporting by Bloomberg News

data.title: Nasa sends 4 astronauts back to Earth in first medical evacuation
data.description:
An astronaut in need of doctors’ care departed the International Space Station with three crewmates on Wednesday in Nasa’s first medical evacuation.
The four returning astronauts – from the US, Russia and Japan – are aiming for an early Thursday morning splashdown in the Pacific near San Diego with SpaceX. The decision cuts short their mission by over a month.
“Our timing of this departure is unexpected,” Nasa astronaut Zena Cardman said before the return trip, “but what was not surprising to me was how well this crew came together as a family to help each other and just take care of each other”.
Officials refused to identify the astronaut who needed care last week and would not divulge the health concerns.
The ailing astronaut is “stable, safe and well cared for”, outgoing space station commander Mike Fincke said earlier this week via social media.
“This was a deliberate decision to allow the right medical evaluations to happen on the ground, where the full range of diagnostic capability exists.”

Launched in August, Cardman, Fincke, Japan’s Kimiya Yui and Russia’s Oleg Platonov should have remained on the space station until late February.
But on January 7, Nasa abruptly cancelled the next day’s spacewalk by Cardman and Fincke and later announced the crew’s early return.
Officials said the health problem was unrelated to spacewalk preparations or other station operations but offered no other details, citing medical privacy. They stressed it was not an emergency situation.
Nasa said it would stick to the same entry and splashdown procedures at flight’s end, with the usual assortment of medical experts aboard the recovery ship in the Pacific. It was another middle-of-the-night crew return for SpaceX, coming less than 11 hours after undocking from the space station.
Nasa said it was not yet known how quickly all four would be flown from California to Houston, home to Johnson Space Centre and the base for astronauts.
One US and two Russian astronauts remain aboard the orbiting lab, just 1½ months into an eight-month mission that began with a Soyuz rocket lift-off from Kazakhstan. Nasa and SpaceX are working to move up the launch of a fresh four-person crew from Florida, currently targeted for mid-February.
Computer modelling predicted a medical evacuation from the space station every three years, but Nasa has not had one in its 65 years of human space flight. The Russians have not been as fortunate.
In 1985, Soviet cosmonaut Vladimir Vasyutin came down with a serious infection or related illness aboard his country’s Salyut 7 space station, prompting an early return. A few other Soviet cosmonauts encountered less serious health issues that shortened their flights.
It was the first space flight for Cardman, a 38, biologist and polar explorer who missed out on spacewalking, as well as Platonov, 39, a former fighter pilot with the Russian Air Force who had to wait a few extra years to get to space because of an undisclosed health issue.
Cardman should have launched last year but was bumped to make room on the way down for Nasa’s Butch Wilmore and Suni Williams, who were stuck nearly a year at the space station because of Boeing’s capsule problems.


Fincke, 58, a retired Air Force colonel, and Yui, 55, a retired fighter pilot with the Japan Air Self-Defense Force, were repeat space fliers.
Finke has spent 1½ years in orbit over four missions and conducted nine spacewalks on previous flights, making him one of Nasa’s top performers.
Last week, Yui celebrated his 300th day in space over two station stays, sharing stunning views of Earth, including Japan’s Mount Fuji and breathtaking auroras.
“I want to burn it firmly into my eyes, and even more so, into my heart,” Yui said on social media. “Soon, I too will become one of those small lights on the ground.”
Nasa officials had said it was riskier to leave the astronaut in space without proper medical attention for another month than to temporarily reduce the size of the space station crew by more than half.
Until SpaceX delivers another crew, Nasa said it will have to stand down from any routine or even emergency spacewalks, a two-person job requiring backup help from crew inside the orbiting complex.
The medical evacuation was the first major decision by Nasa’s new administrator Jared Isaacman. The billionaire founder of a payment processing company and two-time space flier assumed the agency’s top job in December.
“The health and the well-being of our astronauts is always and will be our highest priority,” Isaacman said in announcing the decision last week.

data.title: Hong Kong, China stocks to outpace US equities all year after quick start, analysts say
data.description:
Hong Kong and mainland Chinese stocks outpaced their US peers for the first two weeks of 2026 and should continue to do so for the rest of the year on the back of relatively cheaper valuations, a firmer yuan and policy tailwinds, as geopolitics drives more global investors to look to China as a hedge against rising risks in US assets, according to analysts.
The CSI 300 Index has gained 2.4 per cent so far this year, while the Hang Seng Index has climbed 5.3 per cent, both outpacing a 1.7 per cent gain in the S&P 500. The mainland Chinese and Hong Kong benchmarks jumped 18 per cent and 28 per cent, respectively, in 2025, while the US gauge rose 16 per cent.
Strategists said the re-rating of Chinese stocks was still under way, as their relatively cheaper valuations versus US equities had become increasingly difficult for global investors to ignore.
While major US benchmarks continued to hover near record levels despite years of gains, underlying economic data pointed to a slowdown, according to Tiger Brokers, an online broker backed by shareholders including Xiaomi. This raised the risk that future returns could be capped even if corporate earnings remained resilient, it said.
“The US market is not necessarily at a peak, but it is at a very high position in the cycle,” said Xu Yang, global partner of Tiger Brokers, who added that markets priced at such levels become more sensitive to negative macro surprises.
By contrast, the valuation multiples in Chinese markets had climbed back towards historical medians, which left Chinese stocks “not cheap, but not expensive”, according to Tiger, with room for further gains if corporate earnings began to recover in line with expectations.
The brokerage estimated that over the next five to seven years, average annualised returns for US equities would fall to around 3 to 5 per cent, reflecting stretched valuations, persistent inflation risks and uncertainty over the pace of Federal Reserve rate cuts.
Tiger set a target of 30,000 points for the Hang Seng Index by the end of 2026, representing an upside of 11 per cent from current levels, and 5,000 points for the Shanghai Composite Index, representing a gain of 21 per cent.

UBS Group also struck an upbeat tone on Chinese stocks in 2026, saying China was increasingly viewed by global investors as a hedge against US assets amid stretched valuations and rising policy uncertainty in the world’s largest economy.
The Swiss bank said Chinese equities were supported by improving innovation capabilities, wider adoption of artificial intelligence and potential inflows from both mainland household savings and overseas investors.
KGI Securities, a brokerage unit of KGI Financial Holdings, echoed UBS and Tiger’s views. In its 2026 outlook, the firm said it was time to shift investment strategies from “defensive” to “growth”, with risk appetite recovering and policy support intensifying. It added that some critical signals had been seen, including massive household savings flowing from low-interest fixed deposits into capital markets in search of higher returns.
“Driven by both valuation repair and earnings growth, we are optimistic that the Hang Seng Index will reach 30,000 points, and the allocation value of Hong Kong and mainland China stocks has reappeared,” said Cusson Leung, chief investment officer at KGI.
Additional reporting by Julie Zhang

data.title: Trump praises ‘terrific’ new Venezuela leader after call
data.description:
US President Donald Trump said on Wednesday he had held a “long call” with Venezuela’s interim president Delcy Rodriguez, the first known contact between the two leaders since the ousting of Nicolas Maduro.
“We just had a great conversation today, and she’s a terrific person,” Trump told reporters in the Oval Office.
He later said on social media that he and Rodriguez had discussed “many topics”, including oil, minerals, trade and national security.
“We are making tremendous progress,” Trump said.
After Maduro’s capture in a deadly US special forces operation on January 3, Trump said he was content to let his former deputy Rodriguez take over – as long as she gave the United States access to Venezuelan oil.
He has suggested the United States could maintain oversight of the Caribbean country for years.


Rodriguez has been walking a diplomatic tightrope, trying to meet Trump’s demands without alienating Maduro loyalists, who control Venezuela’s security forces and feared paramilitaries.
Writing on Telegram, she described her call with the US leader as “productive and courteous” and characterised by “mutual respect”.
The 56-year-old added that she and Trump, 79, had discussed a “bilateral work agenda for the benefit of our people, as well as outstanding issues in relations between our governments”.
Earlier, she said at her first press conference as interim president that Venezuela was entering a “new political era” marked by greater tolerance for “ideological and political diversity”.
Under pressure from Washington, Venezuela has released dozens of political prisoners in the past week, but kept hundreds still behind bars.
Rodriguez claimed a total of 406 political prisoners had been released since December in a process that “has not yet concluded”.
The Foro Penal legal rights NGO, which defends many of the detainees, gave a much smaller tally of around 180 freed.
Agence France-Presse’s count, based on data from NGOs and opposition parties, showed 70 people released since the fall of Maduro, who was taken to the United States to face trial for alleged drug trafficking.




Trump has so far sidelined opposition leader Maria Corina Machado from Venezuela’s post-Maduro transition, claiming the Nobel Peace Prize laureate does not have enough “respect” in the country.
Machado, who is currently living outside Venezuela, will meet Trump on Thursday at the White House to press her demands for the opposition to be given a pre-eminent role.
The trickle of prisoner releases continued on Wednesday, with the release of 17 journalists and media workers.
Roland Carreno, a journalist and prominent opposition activist, who was detained in August 2024 during post-election protests, was part of the group.
A leading member of the Popular Will party, he was previously imprisoned between 2020 and 2023 on charges of terrorism – a charge frequently used to lock up opposition members in Venezuela.
In a video shared by another freed journalist, Carreno called for “peace and reconciliation”.
To avoid scenes of jubilant opposition activists punching the air as they walk free from prison, the authorities have been releasing them quietly at other locations, far from the television cameras and relatives waiting outside detention centres.
Carreno was released at a shopping centre. Former presidential candidate Enrique Marquez, one of the first to be released, was driven home in a patrol car.
A US State Department official confirmed on Tuesday that Americans have been released, without saying how many or from where.

data.title: Year of the Horse 2026 predictions for Pigs, from their lucky colour to careers and health
data.description:
From auspicious horse-themed phrases and couplets to whether your luck is in, check out our Year of the Horse 2026 series to discover all you need to know about the coming Lunar New Year.
What does the coming Year of the Horse 2026 have in store for those born in the Year of the Pig (1935, 1947, 1959, 1971, 1983, 1995, 2007, 2019)? Renowned fortune-teller Tong Pik-ha offers her comprehensive predictions for the year.
Lucky numbers: 2, 7; lucky colour: red; lucky element: ruby
Those born in the Year of the Pig will finally escape the effects of clashes with the tai sui, or Grand Duke of Jupiter deity, experienced in the Year of the Snake.
Exiting a volatile year and entering the new one, Pigs will neither conflict nor be in harmony with the tai sui, resulting in a relatively stable overall fortune. Interpersonal relationships and moods will gradually return to normal.
In the new year, those born in the Year of the Pig will be blessed by two auspicious stars related to work and wealth, which means good career development is on the cards. But home fortune remains poor and will require constant attention and vigilance.

Wealth and career
Under the auspicious influence of lucky stars in the Year of the Horse, Pigs can seek new business opportunities, expand their business scope and improve their professional skills to attract more customers.
Furthermore, proactively building good relationships with industry professionals and taking part in business activities or webinars will help increase exposure and expand Pigs’ networks.
Salary earners are likely to gain the appreciation of their superiors or bosses, leading to opportunities for promotion and pay rises.
Things at work will be a lot more harmonious than they were the previous year, so Pigs can expect to start a new chapter. The presence of a benefactor star indicates that interpersonal relationships and networking will be easier.
However, two curse stars are lurking in the charts. One may cause financial losses due to speculation and unexpected circumstances, while the other will cause rumours arising from gossip. Pigs are advised to act discreetly in the workplace to avoid unwanted attention.

Relationships
The Year of the Snake was a pivotal year for Pigs in terms of relationships. Those who experienced relationship changes are likely to meet new partners in the Year of the Horse.
Single women may meet excellent potential partners at work, while single men are advised to participate in social gatherings to meet potential long-term partners.
The good vibes will continue for those who got married or had children in the Year of the Snake. However, Pigs who experienced no pivotal events last year should look at things from their partner’s perspective and work on honest communication.
The influence of the ominous star known as the Death Talisman may cause health problems for family members. Therefore, pay close attention to your partner and do not neglect their feelings due to work commitments.
Health

Things are going to be challenging at home and health-wise for Pigs this year. They should pay close attention to their physical condition. It is also recommended to purchase home insurance and consider minor repairs or furniture replacements to improve the energy at home.
Pigs should also avoid visiting the sick or attending funerals, or going to places like funeral homes and cemeteries, to prevent being affected by bad energy.
Those with chronic illnesses may experience a worsening of their condition. Be sure to take your medication on time. If your condition deteriorates, do not delay treatment.
Are your loved ones in luck this year? See our predictions for all the zodiac signs in the Year of the Horse.

data.title: Hong Kong’s biotech hub aim advanced by pacts with national agencies
data.description:
Hong Kong has taken an important step towards turning itself into an international biotechnology hub with the signing of two landmark agreements with the national health authorities. The pacts deepen collaboration on traditional Chinese medicine and cancer research, and mark the official launch, side by side at Tseung Kwan O, of the Chinese Medicine Hospital and the permanent home of the Government Chinese Medicine Testing Institute, which opened last month.
Their significance was underlined by the presence of the country’s top health official, National Health Commission head Lei Haichao, who called on the city to serve as a pillar of promotion of traditional Chinese medicine. “Hong Kong should leverage its technical strength in Chinese medicine testing and quality control,” he said, while also urging the development of Chinese-Western treatments.
There is no question about demand for the hospital’s services. Hong Kong Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu said the hospital had been so popular with the public it had already increased its service quota three times. That is just the beginning. The national minister also said the hospital positioned Hong Kong as a talent hub for Chinese medicine and helped deepen regional cooperation. He added that the hospital’s opening “is a catalyst to establish a Chinese medicine medical consortium and cluster in the Greater Bay Area” and “should also provide workstations for Chinese medicine masters to attract experts from all over the world”.
What set this week’s ceremony apart were developments to further the hub status of the Chinese Medicine Hospital and testing institution – the signing of contracts to boost collaboration on both Chinese medicine and cancer research.
The city’s Health Bureau and the National Cancer Centre signed up to deepen collaboration in basic cancer research, screening, early diagnosis, standardisation of diagnosis and treatment, and public education. That will come to be seen as a landmark in Hong Kong’s transformation into a biotech hub. Far from being a supplementary treatment, Chinese medicine is increasingly being seen as targeted and less intrusive.
With the signing of these agreements there is potential for collaboration, aided by artificial intelligence, for the more precise diagnosis and targeting of treatment. Already, AI has played a role in this regard on the mainland. Thanks to greater awareness and technological and medical advances, cancer is becoming more curable. Further advances in prevention and cure can depend on comprehensive collaboration across the board with mainland professionals. Hong Kong can play an important role in developing more effective and less intrusive treatments.

data.title: White House says Biden decision to scrap China Initiative flawed
data.description:
The White House’s top science adviser on Wednesday said that the Biden administration’s decision to get rid of the Justice Department’s China Initiative was “damaging”, though stopped short of recommending that it should be fully brought back.
Asked by Florida Republican Daniel Webster whether the termination of the programme aimed at combating alleged economic espionage from Beijing was detrimental, Michael Kratsios, the director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy, said: “It certainly was damaging.”
“We have recognised, and the China Select Committee has also brought to light, a lot of attempted infiltration by nefarious actors of our research enterprise across the Department of War, Department of Energy and many of our other research agencies,” Kratsios said.
He added that it was “important that we remain vigilant in monitoring, tracking and setting up the right safeguards to protect our research ecosystem”, pointing to a Pentagon memorandum from last week that strengthens research security measures against Chinese entities.


The comments were made at a hearing hosted by the House Science Committee, and came just days after House lawmakers stripped language attached to a commerce, justice and science appropriations bill that would have effectively reinstated the initiative.
The Justice Department initiative, launched in 2018 under Donald Trump’s first term, was widely criticised for leading to misguided, racially motivated and excessive prosecutions, and contributing to an exodus of top scientific talent from the US to China.
According to the Department of Justice, only eight of 28 China Initiative cases resulted in convictions or guilty pleas as of August 2021. Four of the convictions or pleas related to academic fraud, and four related to economic espionage and trade theft. As of 2024, an archived department webpage identified 58 cases tied to the China Initiative – a list believed to be incomplete.
The initiative was scrapped by US President Joe Biden’s administration in 2022, with the department pledging to use a “broader approach” beyond just focusing on the potential threat from Beijing.
However, there have been multiple efforts to revive the initiative since then, particularly from Republicans in the House. An accompanying report to the 2026 commerce, justice and science funding bill called for its re-establishment to “maintain America’s competitive edge” and “counter China’s malign ambitions to steal American research”.
Lawmakers, particularly Republicans, have also sought to tighten measures against the US government’s collaboration with Chinese researchers. Last week, months after the House Select Committee on the Communist Party published a report on Pentagon funding of projects involving Chinese research institutions, the Defence Department said it would stop providing fundamental research help to companies it deems linked to the Chinese military.
Kratsios was also asked on Wednesday what steps could be taken to mitigate potential technological risks posed by the presence of roughly 250,000 Chinese students in the US.
Responding to a question by Georgia Republican Rich McCormick, he stressed the use of federal research and development dollars for American scientists and technologists.
“That’s something that we’re going to continue to emphasise as an office and make sure that as we put out [Notices of Funding Opportunity] and other requests for funding, that we’re funding American students.”

data.title: Hong Kong aim to close ‘intensity’ gap, as season hangs in balance at HSBC SVNS 3
data.description:
Jevon Groves has worked his Hong Kong players to the bone ahead of a potentially watershed weekend in Dubai, one year after the city team “couldn’t stand the intensity” of Challenger Series rugby.
Hong Kong must reach Sunday’s HSBC SVNS 3 final to advance to the next stage, a three-leg competition over February and March that will provide four tickets to the inaugural HSBC SVNS World Championship Round.
But as much as Groves, the head coach, is keen to stave off the possibility of no serious rugby until September’s Asian Games, he has fostered a unified desire to “prove we’re much better than we showed last year”.
In the 2025 Challenger Series, the forerunner of HSBC SVNS 3, Hong Kong lost all four group games over two legs and finished ninth. Rather than ban talk of that experience for fear of reopening mental wounds, Groves has used it as a motivational tool for one of the most pivotal tournaments of his nine-year reign.
“We’re not harping on about qualifying … we need to reset and give a true representation of ourselves, which we didn’t do last year for multiple reasons we’ve tried to address,” Groves said. “One was that the players felt they couldn’t stand the intensity going from Asian competitions to the Challenger, and we have tried to bridge that gap.
“We want to be competitive and a team no one wants to play.”
After Hong Kong claimed Asia series and National Games titles late last year, Groves and his staff “went pretty hard at the boys” during a five-week camp, with the onus on conditioning.
With physical Tonga and Samoa teams among their group opponents on Saturday, along with unknown quantity Colombia, the additional “hard work on our tackle and collision” should serve Groves’ side well.

A top-two pool finish would book a high-stakes last-four meeting with one of Belgium, Italy, Canada or Madagascar the following day; the winners sealing a final place and, more importantly, HSBC SVNS 2 qualification.
Groves said the outcome would be especially significant because “we want to play in this bracket of tournament as consistently as possible”.
“It would also create opportunities for other players to come in ... we could rotate and build the depth we’ll need if players move on, get injured or are called up for 15s.”
The 15s has grown into a tricky subject following Hong Kong’s qualification for next year’s Rugby World Cup, given the number of players who compete in both codes.
“It’s just another obstacle and challenge we have to manage,” Groves said. “I would never try to take a World Cup away from a player; when it comes round we have to actively embrace it.
“It will highlight our need for a wider squad, we have lots of players knocking on the door, and it would be great to expose them to this level.”
Liam Doherty is recovering from ACL surgery, but Groves was confident a “resilient character”, man of the match when Hong Kong beat South Korea for Asian Games gold in 2023, would recover for this year’s Games.
He otherwise reported a clean bill of health for Dubai, although acknowledged that “we’ve been well prepared before and come unstuck in our first game”.
As such, Groves said his team were “fully focused on delivering a performance we can be proud of” in their opener against Tonga. All three group matches will be crammed inside roughly 6½ hours.
“It’s much easier to recover and review after a win and good performance,” he said. “It’s hard to measure, but you generate tournament momentum. After a loss, it’s a tough skill to wrestle back that confidence and momentum.
“If we get through it will set us up for the first half of the year; along with gaining redemption after last year, that is our motivation.”
Hong Kong squad: James Christie (co-captain), Seb Brien (co-captain), Callum McCullough, Alessandro Nardoni, Liam Herbert, Jack Combes, Julien Bourron, Bryn Phillips, Harry Sayers, Matt Rickard, Fong Kit-fung, Max Denmark.
